These are sort of broad questions that were not asked specifically. And then I'm going to get into the list of questions that were asked specifically. These are sort of compiling questions together. So one big question that seems to be coming up a lot is like, how do I start? Right? You know, what's sort of the minimum viable? Do I need a full multi-page pre-sell site and everything built out? And then, you know, from there we move forward. And the reality is this is a hard question to answer because if you have an offer that has never converted, meaning no one's ever bought it, then you have to have to, it was the wrong phrase. You really should figure out if you can sell that in the minimum viable way to sell that. So whatever that is, and if it's, you know, and you should sell it online. So if you can sell it to your 10 best friends, that's different than if you can put up something that allows people to buy it and get them to buy it. Now, I would not in that circumstance say, let me just think out loud for a second here, like minimum viable for your, whatever you're doing is going to be different. If it's a membership, then there's sort of a lot of moving parts. If it's a one-off, then there are fewer moving parts. If you already have, I just, I don't know your situation, but what I don't want anybody to do, and this is so easy to do, but I don't want people to spend weeks, months, years building out an entire structure of an offer with zero validation. Like you have no idea if anybody's going to buy it. So what we want to try to figure out how to do, and I can't tell you how to do this specifically for your situation. There are just too many variables. So you have to really, you have to hear sort of the framework that I'm talking about, which is what is minimum viable for you? So if it is, you have a hundred and ninety, you have some of you want to sell for $197, it's reasonably complete, and you can throw up a sales page. Well, throw up a sales page and see if anybody buys it, send some traffic to it, send really specific traffic, particularly search traffic, that is bullseye search terms. It's clear that it's a perfect match. And if nobody buys it, don't build out an enormous amount of infrastructure to try to figure out why no one bought it in the first place. We want minimum infrastructure, and you can use a landing page service, like an unbalance, or you can use like, blasphemy, I use it, you can use click funnels if you need to, like whatever you need to use to get something up to test if somebody will buy it. You need to answer the will people buy it question before you build out a tremendous amount of assets. Now, if you don't have that yet, and I'm going to talk a little and trying to avoid talking about Facebook until we get to Facebook, but there is some Facebook conversation we need to have, and there's a question about this later, which is if you don't have the offer dialed in yet, what do you do? There is a testing framework that's module, so I sort of call it module 6.5 instead of calling it another master class, but there is a way to test with Facebook. So, but when we do that, we're not testing a the most sophisticated system possible with, you know, four page multi page pre-sell site and seven emails and an offer with eight modules, we're not doing that, we're testing an idea because we need a signal early. So long answer, but I see a lot of these questions about well, how do I know this is going to be successful? I, we don't, most things fail. So we need, if we're going to fail, we need to fail fast and fail forward. Okay. All right. That leads me to this, you know, what's the definition of Google ad success right now where you are in the program? This has been a lot of questions around this. The definition of success right now, meaning today or between before you actually start anything with Facebook, assuming you have something that you can send traffic to, if that's true for you, success right now is one bullseye keyword phrase that's getting 10 clicks a day. That's success. Don't make it more complicated than that. I see a lot of people in the comments, a lot of the emails I'm getting that's like taking something that simple and making it 25 times more complex. Don't do that. Make it simple. Keep it simple. And if we will build on the success of simplicity, we're not going to try to figure out how to unwind the problems of complexity. It's too hard. If you throw 100 keywords into an ad group and push the button, you're going to be spending so much money so fast and you will never get a clear signal. One bullseye keyword phrase, 10 clicks a day, that's the goal. Now, many of you said, well, I might not get 10 clicks a day. What about that? That's okay. Five clicks a day, if that's what you need. But what I'm trying to get you to focus on is this minimum viable idea to dip your toe into the Google ads pool and in a way that you will understand how it works. And all it takes to do that is one really good highly focused keyword phrase that's getting some traffic. And once you have that, that will, that is the kernel that will allow you to learn everything you need to know about AdWords. Google ads, I think I mentioned earlier, I'm going to say AdWords the whole time. So, okay, anything to add to these things, André? I know you have some thoughts on minimum viable too. Yeah, I don't really have much to add to what you've already said. I think if somebody doesn't have an offer, there's a lot of different ways they can test. And one of the easiest ways, I guess, in the beginning would just be to see if they can get an action, you know, any action. If they can't get opt-ins, or somebody to fill out an application form or some survey thingy, then you're not going to be able to sell anything. So, you know, sticking up a stick in a bar button on there is probably going to be less useful. So, I think it's just figure out what that action is that you want to test and then test that. Obviously, if you already have a have a proven offer, then then it's a different game. But yeah, I think that's that's all really cool. All right. So, there have been some questions about scaling. And and I think I think the questions are coming partly from looking through the lens of the world through the Facebook lens of the world. And we'll talk about scaling on Facebook later. Facebook has some some weird issues. I think they're weird. They have they have some some strange issues with scaling, like you can't scale too much too fast. And Lauren will speak to that far better than I do about what what those parameters look like. Google's not that way at all. And the questions have sort of raised a little bit of concern with me that I didn't explain myself well. When you have want let's let's kind of go back to the goal here. If you have one keyword phrase and you are tracking conversions and you know the results and you're spending what's just say for the sake of argument, you're spending $50 a day. And you can see that when you spend $50 a day on that keyword phrase, you're getting $100 in sales. And then in Google says in its estimator, you could spend $1,000 a day because there's enough search traffic for this term. How much should you spend per day? Well, nothing's going to change. Why it's there's no there's nothing to suggest that when you get 10 people or whatever it is 20 people searching for the exact same thing that the next 100 people searching for the exact same thing are going to be different. So there there is there's nothing should make you believe that if you scale results, if you if you've structured your account the right way, and you scale results that your results are somehow going to go sideways, because the the results are the results like if the search term is the search term. Now my concern in the question though is that where my explanation didn't land the way I intended it, which is there if you have an ad group or ad groups that have one or two keyword phrases that are actually ROI positive, you're making money on those, and then you have several others that aren't, and you scale that well now you have a problem because you just scaled things that are not working at the same and now we don't know what's going to happen because it may very well be that the the thing that's driving results that's getting what you want that has only a certain amount of volume, and all the things that you don't want have far greater volume in terms of search search terms. So then when you crank the dial from $50 a day to $1,000 a day, you only turned up the good by a factor of two, and you turned up the bad by a factor of 20. And now everything goes sideways. And I think that's what's happening with these questions. So I want to go back to the very beginning and say your ad group should have a single keyword phrase in it. Anytime you scale, you're only scaling that single keyword phrase because then you know the extent. So put one keyword phrase per ad group for now so that when you scale that, you know exactly what your scale, you're getting all signal, no noise. And will somebody volunteer, someone who asks this question volunteered to have me do an actual analysis of an account, and I'm going to do that and I'm going to share that with everybody. It'll be in the academy where I will go through and see this person who asked the question and is offered to share the account has a profitable account with Google ads at a low volume. And in the past when they've scaled it, it has gone off the rails financially. And I suspect it's gone off the rails because there's a lot of noise with the signal and both are being scaled. And that's hypothesis. So everybody will see that. I'm thank you. I will thank, I want to have confirmed with the person that they're okay with me sharing this with everybody. I'll thank that person by name. I don't want to mention his or her name right now. Okay. The question. I have a question. Let me raise my hand. Jump the queue. So if you've got the one ad group with the one keyword, presumably ideally it's an exact match phrase you would want inside there or modified broad? Oh, we did modify broad with negatives. So you potentially do have or you could have the misspellings in the same ad group? Yeah, Google manages misspellings pretty well now. Synonyms misspelling in the past. Yeah, even exact match now Google is a little looser about its interpretation and generally okay. Like broad match, not so much modified broad match exact phrase. The search terms you're getting, they tend to match there. They match with intent. Gotcha. Okay. So just taking that question further even though you have one, one specific keyword search phrase, you really don't, you have more, you have the ingredients of one. And when you use the search terms report, you'll see that they're performing. There is, there is some nuance in here, but the underlying message is that we have to have this super focus because the moment we lose that focus, it's so easy to have the things that are actually causing drag scale up way faster than the things that are producing the result that we want. And we just have to be able to see how that happens because it may happen for a day. We may just, we may go from 50 to $1,000 a day and see a performance drop, but we need to be able to go in and say, well, why? Oh, okay, here's this thing that happened. Let me turn that off with negatives or whatever it is. And that's just part of the game. But my hope in answering this question is that we should design the system so it doesn't produce that result. Or if it does produce that result, it doesn't produce that result for very long and we know how to remedy it. That's the goal. Okay. Next question. This is a question I've heard for 20 years. It's a question I've dealt with for 20 years. It's probably the most difficult part of Google ads. What if there isn't much search volume? Facebook's advertising platform kind of exists because of this question. I mean, this is, this has always been the challenge with Google ads. There's only so much there, there, right? There are only so many profitable keyword phrases. And you, when you, you, you start, there's sort of concentric circles, you get the first group and those are profitable. And you think, Oh, this is excellent. And then the next group, they're a little farther away, sort of conceptually, and they're either less profitable or it's harder to make them profitable. And at some point, you just run out. And that's just the reality of it. I mean, there's a, there's a third party tool I've mentioned is called spy foo. Spy foo has this really interesting function called combat spelled with a K where they take, you can take three competitors and it will spy foo goes out and grabs all three competitors list of keywords they're bidding on and then it puts them in event, a Venn diagram, where do the individuals overlap with each other and we're all three overlap in the middle where they overlap. Those are the keyword phrases that they're all bidding on. And one of the most shocking things you learn when you do that is that there aren't generally 50 to 100 keywords that established advertisers are all bidding on, which generally means that there's, there's money to be made there. That's where the profit ability is. The rest is sort of just maybe unique to each one in some individual way, but that, that, that core overlap, there just isn't a lot there. And it's, there's just no more to get. Like that's, that's the challenge with search volume is it's, it is, there's a certain size audience actively looking for what you have to offer. And, and there's no real way to make that bigger. So we start with Google ads because that we want to go after, we want to get that initial active intent search volume, but we do that knowing it will run out. Unfortunately, I mean, some businesses are built entirely on search, some businesses can be built on search. If you have that, it's sort of a need-based business that there's always a volume of search. The beauty of search though, is that it's a, the pool tends to refresh a lot. Right. So, you know, there's sort of a daily or weekly or whatever you want to think about it, volume. And it's sort of new and refreshed every day. So every day that, you know, we sort of reset the counter and we've got, we have another opportunity and off we go. And that's a little different than, than Facebook conceptually. Facebook's audiences are generally bound by the number of individuals in the audience. So you can go to that audience and, and really sort of exhaust an audience in a way with Facebook that you don't with Google because Google refreshes every day. So each has their own thing. That's why the traffic engine has both, because the two of them together do things that are so different yet complimentary, that if you use both, you can capture such a broad spectrum of intent and interest and other things. So that's sort of, there in many ways a perfect combination. And in a small stroke of irony, I have a much greater appreciation for Facebook now having worked through all the challenges of Google ads. So, yeah, that was not expected by any stretch. And I've so in love with Google ads again, because it's excellent. Okay. I think those are the questions. Those were the initial questions that I sort of compiled. How do we link the results of the exercise, jobs, pains and gains with our actual Google and Facebook ads? So if you look back in the module, or I'm sorry, in part two of the masterclass, we go through the value proposition design canvas exercise. I mentioned toward the end of that, that you should create this document where you compile that sort of this creative brief, a messaging brief. So go back to the module and reread that section because what we're doing when at the end of the value proposition design canvas exercise, we want to have a document that says, here's my audience. Here's what I believe the top one to three jobs, one to three pains and one to three gains are. And you can mix and match those. You may have an audience that it's all focused on jobs and there are three of them might be all pains, three of them or it could be a pain again and a job. And you just think about this conceptually. But that should be the core to inform your messaging. So it is the phrase I'm borrowing from Donald Miller, the author and now marketer. It's he calls a controlling idea. So those are your controlling ideas. So if you have, let's say you have two jobs, two pains, two gains that you have determined matter most to your audience. When you write your ad copy, whether it's for Facebook or Google, those are the only things that you're writing about. That's all the messaging is around that. That's those are the controlling ideas. So if you have, if you find yourself writing ad copy, and it's not about one of those jobs or one of those pains or one of those gains, it doesn't belong. It does not matter how important it is. If it's not important to that particular audience, it does not belong in that ad for that audience period. That becomes your those become your controlling ideas. This is the, and the best way to do that is just have a document where you put those things and then ask yourself what you can ask questions like what did you know how what is about my product that addresses that particular job or what is it about my offer that deals with that gain or pain or speak to the pain or the gain and reinforce it and hint at a solution. But those are the only things that you talk about. And you can have a single document that has, you know, has this by audience. So it'll say, you know, here's the audience. And then here are the jobs, pains and gains for that audience. And then here's the next audience. And here is a slightly different remix of those. I don't know if I mentioned this specifically in trying to remember if I if I included this or not. But when I did this once with a with a client where we thought we really thought they had one audience. And as in the morning, the challenge came from we couldn't narrow the list down. There were like 10 jobs and, you know, six pains and six, and some of them, you know, there was a there were some that were shared. But what we finally figured out is that there were three audiences, and they all looked very similar. But what made them different is the job. I did include this as an example. Actually, now I remember what made them different is the jobs that were most important. So who they reported to, for example, did they have to report to somebody like that, that became such a differentiator that that's what we had to speak to. And that's really what you're getting when you create this messaging brief. It's who am I speaking to and what matters most to her. Like that's really what you're trying to have. And sometimes you can do that at the audience level. And sometimes the audience is just a search term. And you don't know really as much about the person, but you know implied in the search term is a pain or a gain or a job to be done. And then you just speak to that. So that's a long answer to the question. But I hope and we can if there's a different additional clarification, just drop it in the Q&A. And we'll jump back on that. All right, let's see. All right, next group. This is this is a group of questions from someone. First question one, sometime ago, all classes about Google ads always started with all these minimum quality aspects that you need on your web page in order to get approved, having about us page having a page about your data policy, etc. Is that still a case or did they relax? Yes, that is still the case. Yes, you should. I will include a link if I haven't already to Google sort of page standards, but you need to have contact information, privacy policy, terms of service. And it's think less about like what's the least I need to do to get past Google's, you know, needs. And what should I do to make my audience feel more comfortable with my content? So if you visit a page and there's no about there's about us, there's no contact info, there's no privacy policy, there's no, there's nothing other than the content on the page, how likely are you to believe that page is credible? Less likely, I believe is the answer. So forget about Google, think about your user, do what's best for the user and what's best for the user is some transparency around who you are, what you do, how you do it and how it relates to them. So that's the lens through which I would look. Second part of this question is also at that time it was pretty much prohibited to send traffic to a squeeze page, does that still apply? Yeah, Google doesn't like thin content. And Google doesn't love gated content either. Go back to module three, so that covered that in a few sections where if you're optimizing for the user experience and thin content pages where it's just hey, there's this thing and give me your email and I'll tell you what it is, those days are gone, long gone, regardless of what Google likes, users hate them. Put content that is relevant and you'll get and so this is a and everybody loses situation. The user doesn't like it, Google doesn't like it and your results will suffer. So all three things point in the wrong direction. Send people to content that answers the question that they asked in their search query. That's the starting point. It's almost like a conversation where someone has said to you, I am really interested in X, whatever X is, you would not say, oh, you know what, I've spent a long time thinking about that question too, but before I tell you what I've discovered, could you please give me your email address? That's not how human beings communicate. Those days are gone. You need to think about instead is in that person says, here's something that I'm looking for, establish empathy and credibility first. Let them know that you've heard the search term, you're giving them some amount of content related to that, like to let them know you are in the right place. And at that point, after you've given them enough information to confirm that you have empathy with their search, that you understand it, and that you have authority and credibility to show them a way to find the answer that they're looking for, then you can ask for them to take the next step. And you can say, this is something that's better covered over several days. I do that by email. I have you can do this thing and just sign up here. That's perfectly okay. But the thin squeeze content squeeze page days are over. And they will they will try to do that will make you crazy. All right, let's move on. Question two, I did not quite understand under recommendation for timing for the initial run. So we would switch out 12am and 5am and test performance of weekends versus weekdays. Okay, just to clarify, there was a lot of confusion around that. Those were examples of what you can do, not suggestions for what you should do. I have no idea if your offer converts better or worse overnight during the day, during the weekdays, during weekends, I don't know. I can't know that you have to figure that out. What my intent was in sharing that is to let you know that it is figure outable. And it is a factor. So geography, keywords, and time of day, day of week are factors that increase or decrease the probability of success for you. So I have clients that are business to business, for example, they sell things to other businesses, they do not advertise in the weekends, because they just know historically from their data that any traffic they get on the weekend is far less likely to convert. That may be true for you, it may not be true for you. What I want you to think about is stacking the deck in your favor. So if you initially do that with a good focus keyword search phrase, and then do it with geography, most definitely. And beyond that, I would be less, I would concern myself less with day of week, time of day, but later go back and know that those are factors that you can use to make things a little more profitable. You're not probably not going to change the economics 100%, but you might change the economics 20%. So that there are other factors that you can start looking at. Don't feel like you need to do this right out of the gate, just know that it is something that you can do. Question three from the same person, you mentioned that for your ad for the university and ad copy, adding a sense of urgency made all the difference. Is that a specific example of how studying your competitors can help you find relevant elements and structure that you should include into your ads? Or do you in general recommend adding a sense of urgency? First part. That was just an example of something that I found common to high performance ads in that very specific narrow field. That is not a suggestion to add urgency to everything that you do. But it's the idea in that section of the module was that we can begin our ads, we can create our ads, our starting point can be representative of the best that somebody else has figured out for the last two years. We can start where they are instead of starting where they were and then spending two years to get to our best performing ad. Somebody else has done the work for testing already. Go figure out what they did, infer what you can infer from it and start there. Don't copy their ads that it's not what we're trying to do, but what we're trying to look and see is like, what is it about this ad that is making it so valuable to the audience? Why is this ad working? Or why did they settle on this as a control? If it's been around for a long time, because we know that we can find that out, we should find that out, and we should use that information to improve our ads instead of trying to reinvent the wheel and start from the completely blank slate, which is what it feels like a lot when you're writing ad copy. You sit down in Google ads and you have no idea even where to start. Let's go look and see what the best performing ad is right now. Let's start there. Let's see what that structure looks like. If that's available, sometimes it's just someone's running with the same ad for a long period of time and it's not a sophisticated advertiser and there's nothing other than the fact that they're in the number one position to tell us that that ad is valuable, and not just the ad, the landing page as well, that the whole thing is valuable. Let's go figure out something that an audience has raised their hand and said this combination of ad and landing page is valuable. We know that in the number one spot. Let's go extract some wisdom from that and see where that leads. Okay, Andre, anything to just jump in here? I'm running my app. I tend to go fast. So anything before I jump into the next set of questions? You're in a roll. All right, too much coffee this morning. All right, this is a question I think I got this in like 12 places and I'm psyched the person who asked it is on the call so I get to finally answer it. Here's the question. You say to turn off mobile tablet traffic and focus on desktop traffic. I just checked my stats from 1st April until today and 75% of my website visitors on mobile and tablet and most by on mobile devices. I just need some clarity and double checking. Even though I say this is not always true and your results may vary, however, initially we want to control as many factors as possible. Later we can incrementally add mobile and tablet traffic and measure those results separately. Should I be preparing to only focus on desktop as it seems I will be missing a huge chunk of traffic? So your first paragraph, there are two parts to the paragraph and I want to answer them separately in case other people I want to make sure that everybody hears the nuance in this. So the first part says I just checked my stats from 1st April until today and 75% of my website visitors are on mobile and traffic. If that's true for you, just that, which is very common. If that's true for you, do not assume that you should advertise from mobile and tablet just because that's where your traffic comes from. Now the second part of the question and most by on mobile devices, that's actually the key phrase. If you have data that says people buy from you on mobile, then ignore what I said about filtering out mobile because you already know that you're getting buyers from mobile. However, pay attention to it. See where your buyers are coming from to see if this holds true when you're using Google ads as well. Some markets people buy and increasingly people buy with mobile. But in general, from a probabilistic standpoint, if you look broadly at 100 different products, 100 different offers, chosen at random, put them all in a box and say let's look at the data for all of these desktop versus mobile versus tablet, desktop will outperform the others by factors of eight or nine to one just because it's, some of it's simple, just mashing the buttons to put payment information into your phones isn't always easy. I suspect this is less true outside of the United States, particularly any place where mobile is certainly a part of life in the United States. I don't want to suggest it's not, but mobile and buying a little less in the US, I don't know why that is. So if you're in a country and people buy stuff using their mobile device at some higher rate or normal rate versus desktop, that's going to change everything that I said. What I'm hoping to get to when there's a little bit of nuance where let's turn off the things that in general can drag down our results unless you have data that suggests otherwise, in which case your data always is the source of the best insight. So long answer to a very, very important question, be guided by your data but check all the time. You may have a phenomenon where organic traffic performs one way and you can't replicate how that works with paid. That happens, just be aware that you should be checking to see where the sales are coming from when you're paying for traffic. And it may be a simple matter of just dropping the mobile bid pricing down by a factor like down to 60%. So you'll find your level in there. Okay. I've got a question about that. So at the campaign level for his particular use case where his data is suggesting that you do something which is not the default play that you would suggest. So would you suggest in that use case that at the campaign level he has two campaigns for the exact same keyword phrases and just ones turned on for mobile, ones turned on for desktop? Yeah, we used to have to do that now. Google sort of changed the way they do things. You can effectively turn it off but you can't. The way we used to do it, I know what you're thinking about because I know you did this too, is we would have a campaign that was such and such search dash mobile tablet and then we would turn off desktop traffic. And then you would have the same campaign but then it would be desktop, you turn off mobile and tablet. What Google has done instead is they've put everything in the same campaign and you have a, for mobile and desktop, you have a multiplier plus or minus by percentage and then the data is shown, the data is broken out that way, you can see it. So you don't have to do it that way anymore. He can have one campaign that has both where he just adjusts the pricing multiplier for mobile and for desktop and then looks at those results to find sort of that sweet spot. And he may find that mobile outperforms. It's worth paying more for. You can do 140% bid. So is that, explain that well? Yeah, yeah. That's good. So you can pay, you can essentially pay different prices even for the same keywords based on the platform that they're using or the device that they're using. So even though desktop is 25% of the traffic, it might still, in relation, produce more sales relatively even though. The economics will be different too. That's what you'll see is like, and this is where we get this weird nuance of Google ads. It's beautiful when you understand it. And for those of you who are, this is just a quick interjection. So if you're not understanding it at the level at which I'm explaining it right now and that is bothering you, don't worry. You're on the road to understand it. But keep in mind, I have a two decade head start. So when I'm just throwing these things out the way I see the world and how I can sort of look at it 12 different ways, 20 years is a long time. And tens of millions of dollars of ads, it's a lot. So what I've given in module three in particular, in module four as well is if you want to see the world the way I see the world within relationship to Google ads, that's the framework. That is how I see the world. As you practice that framework, specific to your business or if you work with clients, you will begin to see, you'll begin to make the second and third order conclusions that I make sort of naturally on the fly. It's just a matter of practice. And as you practice it, and that's why I structured that module to be so disfluent, like we do everything manually, we go in and we monkey around with all these different settings manually, even though many of them are managed very well by Google, I still want you to know where the settings are and what it does when you mash the buttons, because that is teaching you everything I suggested teaches you something that's critically important about how Google ads work. So you are by the virtue of doing, following that outline, you're learning ad words in a really interesting way that's going to pay huge dividends over time. Okay, next question, same person, am I better trying to sell the idea, for example, a lead via the advertiser or the product itself? Let me see. Oh wait, no, sorry, there was a question before this. Okay, I don't know, this person says, I don't have a website yet. It's a landing page to gather email addresses. I usually do this for Facebook, lead generation to an email series, to a sales page, or Google ads work with directing traffic this way, or should I spend the time building a manifesto, for example, a multi-page pre-sell site, plus about contact pages on the website, what's the leanest version I can get away with to get started? In the second part of the question, I better try to sell the idea like a lead magnet via the advertisement of the product itself. So I don't know, that's the honest answer, because I don't know the product, I don't know the audience, I don't know any of the details that could actually, I could have an informed opinion. So let me try to answer the question in the absence of specific knowledge. Sending Google traffic to a page to get their email addresses, I covered that a little bit earlier. It really depends on how much content is on the page. I do not think you need to spend it, well, let me take a step back. If this is something that you have, that is an offer that is generating customers, it's proven, then is it worth the time building out a multi-page pre-sell site? I think it is, again, depending on your specific situation. I don't know, and now if you have somebody, this is sort of a peri-martial term, if you have somebody with a search and they have a bleeding neck, that's peri-martial's term, I think it was peri, or he quoted somebody where they need the thing, if your roof is leaking, I use the roof leaking one all the time, it's a bleeding neck. If you are standing in your living room at 4 a.m. with water pouring into your house and you have just searched for roofing contractor near me, you have a bleeding neck problem and you do not need a four-page multi-page pre-sell site to solve it. You need a phone number and you need to know that it's 24-hour service and they're going to be there in a half an hour. That's all you care about. So then the texture of the search term, the texture of the product that you have and that the problems that you solve dramatically affects how much content needs to be on the landing page. Most things are not like that. Most things are not leaky roofs or they're things that are like you're interested in garden design and you're thinking about maybe spending the money to have landscapers come in and design a backyard for you. Well, in that case, I don't know that I would do a manifesto per se, but I might do three or four pages about all of the things somebody needs to know or many of the things somebody needs to know to ask really good questions before they and to really have a high desire to get on a design consultation call. That's what I would build into that sort of front end because I know they have a need. They want to talk to somebody about garden design, but I also know that it would be helpful to them and to me if I sort of change their worldview a little bit and let them know these are the four or five things to consider and before you get on a call, this is what we would cover. That's beneficial. So really hard to answer this question without knowing the exact context, but the underlying answer to the question is you don't want to build out every conceivable asset without knowing that the thing that you are doing works. If you know the things that you are doing works, then I would begin to layer on things to make it work better. The first thing that you can layer on to something that works to make it work better is high quality traffic. So if you've got an offer that converts and you have a sense of who bought it and why they bought it, go find those people and sell it more to them, pay to sell to them. That's really the and then it's just you add a little bit, add a little bit, add a little bit until, you know, and yes, there are some minimums. Yes, you need to have a landing page that has contact information, that has some identity of who you are, that has terms of service, has privacy. Yes, you need those things because those are the right things to do. And so that's the leanest version, I think. And then this corollary question is, are you better to sell the idea like a lead magnet or the product itself? This is a really, really good question. There are methodologies and there are, well, yeah, so there are methodologies and there are sort of ways to think about selling things that I understand intellectually why the lead happens first. But if you don't know that if you don't know that you can sell the thing, to me, it's a really scary high risk position to put yourself in to do a lead magnet and spend a bunch of money on traffic to that lead magnet. So the example I'm thinking about, of course, is product launch formula, Jeff Walker's approach. I have nothing against PLF, if anyone loves it or Jeff's your best friend, I'm not speaking ill at all about Jeff Walker or PLF, but there is a fundamental assumption in product launch formula, which is that gathering leads is going to lead to sales. And I have seen launches gather lots of leads and have few or no sales. So there is not, even though it seems like there's a causal connection, like you can't buy, if you didn't become a lead, I've seen far too many examples where becoming a lead had no relationship to buying. So I don't love lead magnets if they're a way to distract from proving the thing that actually matters. So I would rather see all things being equal. I would rather see somebody put together a sales page and sell the thing and prove that they can sell the thing to a subset of an audience that is most responsive. And if you can sell the thing, then figure out other ways to move your audience closer to the buying of the thing through email, series, all of that, do all of that. And there's a sensible order to do that. But don't convince yourself that you're not sure if you can sell the thing. So let's then, let's go do what's generate leads instead, and then we'll figure out how to sell the thing. That's a dangerous game. I've seen that game lead to really horrible results for people, like spending lots of money to find out that the thing they wanted to sell doesn't sell. That's no fun. All right, I'm going to take a drink of water here before the next one. Andre, anything to throw in there? No, I'm, yeah. You're so laid back and quiet today. Like, I'll just, let me give him some rope and see if he hangs himself. It'll be fun. I have a conversion tracking problem. Don't we all, brother? I sell on a partner site and I have no pixel there. I have tried offline conversions on Facebook and they're poor. If there are workarounds, I am keen to hear about them for Facebook or Google. This is an incredibly, this problem is so common. It's, it is, there is no, I mean, there are sometimes they're like crazy and depending on your specific situations there, you know, maybe I don't want to rule it out, but when you sell somewhere else and you, you are sending traffic somewhere in between and you don't have, it's like, we run into a million different problems. First, there are attribution problems. Right. So, and I don't want to go down the rabbit hole of attribution because I'm certain that there are many of you on the call who are smarter about attribution than I am and I'll say something, you'll tell me how wrong I was, but conceptually speaking, here's a challenge with attribution. Somebody goes from Google or Facebook to whatever landing page you have control over that you have tracking on, on a certain domain or subdomain and then, and Google Analytics sees where they came from. It's, they can attribute the source. Then someone clicks from that to a partner site. You've just broken attribution. So now there's no connection between what they originally clicked on and then the click in between. So you, you can see that you got a hundred people from Google click to your website and then in a hundred people from Facebook click to your website. And then you can see 60 people total went to the partner site if you have any tracking there, but you have no idea if they were from Google or if they were from Facebook, you've lost that attribution. That's, that's, it's a miserable problem. And there are tools that try to solve this problem. And if you, if you own all of the pieces of the public, a puzzle you can do cross domain tracking and it is potentially solvable, but the way you've described the problem is really the extreme of the, the, what I think is essentially an unsolvable problem. If you pay for traffic to one location and you sell it that you can track and you sell in another location, another digital location that you can't track, there, there really is no way to know what's working and it becomes exceptionally difficult to, to track that, to, to infer anything from that. So I don't, I don't have a great answer for this. I think a lot of it comes down to like, how does your partner, like how does the partner site work? Like that's a big challenge. Do they, could you drop a GTM container on there and maybe, maybe do some tracking? I mean, that's where I would look. They're GTM Google tag manager. I mean, there might be, you might be able to drop your container on that site and sort of find a word, there might be something in there. Well, some of the things that I used to do when I used to be an affiliate for, for other places. When I look at my email content, mobile wins by a mile. Reading email is not an analog to buying products. There's no causal, even if you say there's no causal, some clicks on links and buys, there is a cause, right? People, I do this all the time. I read on my mobile device, I read in the grocery, back before quarantine, I might be standing in line at the grocery store, reading somebody's email, I'm not buying on my phone. If I buy, I buy from the desktop. Now did the email influence me? Yes. But email content and consumption of email content is not an analog to buying behavior. So don't confuse those two things. Now at the same time, don't take what I've said to disconfirm the possibility that you are absolutely right. If you, if the majority of your email content is consumed on a mobile device and your site is optimized for mobile and everything for you lines up that prioritizes mobile, you're probably right. Mobile probably is a better option for you, but make sure you test mobile against desktop and laptop in tablet. Just be aware that those are three different, are there a set of variables over which you have control and a set of variables over which you should analyze relative to the behavior that you care about most, which I suspect is buying. See, this is like knowing your audience is such a great thing. It's just thinking about a very narrow lens. And making a mistake, which I want to say we all make this, but I'll just I'll personalize it. I make this mistake and I make it consistently, which is assuming that a lot of the world behaves the same way that I do. Now, I've fortunately built into my thinking the habit of always testing my thinking. So if I think mobile's going out or mobile's going to underperform, I at least check it. And that's really what I want to instill in all of you. That again, all things being equal, 100 businesses, you're going to find that mobile underperforms. But all things aren't equal. Your your business is a unique little flower and you need to figure this out for yourself. But you need, again, you need to know it's figure out. And that's where I want you to focus. And I want you to understand why I think this way. But I don't want you to think the way I don't want you to think what I think. I want you to think how I think. And then your results will be terrible to your specific situation. All right, next question. Geography, same person. Geography tiers make sense. If, for example, the UK is my biggest market, I could bid $10. And then France, let's say, converted France converted half as well, would you then bid $5? So proportional bids based on results, I can see the UK is tier one, big European countries tier two, and then the rest of the US tier three, not that's not really what I'm getting at. The adjusting the bid isn't really what I'm getting at. I don't think maybe it is. And when I finish describing it, I'll realize that's exactly what I was saying. What I'm trying to get at here is not so much how much can you bid, but where should you spend your if you have a finite amount of advertising dollars, where is the highest return on investment going to come from? So if in the example that you gave the UK is your biggest market, I don't I don't know that that necessarily matters. Unless the UK is the your market where you are most likely to produce a sale. In that case, that should be tier one. If you're a little less likely to produce a sale in France, or, you know, France, Spain, or Germany, then that should be tier two, then the rest of your tier three. But the factor that matters isn't the size of the market. It is the likelihood of the market to buy. So in the examples that I gave, like the what the writing retreat that I mentioned, tier what made tier one, tier one was that we believed people were more likely to buy because of geographic proximity. That was that factor mattered. Tier two, we believe they were less likely to buy than tier one, more likely to buy than tier three. So if we when we were looking at spending ad money, we wanted to spend all the money we could on tier one, because we believe that that audience had the highest likelihood of buying in it. And they were worth more that every dollar we put into spend there was worth more. I can be keep this pronouncing the same word was worth more than dollars that we would spend on on tier two. So for your example, the filter you need to ask yourself is not where is the market by size, it is where are where am I most likely to get a customer. And if it's the UK, then that's what that should be your tier one. And then you just after that look where are you next most likely to get a customer. And you may need to do it by country to really answer those questions. And then then at that point where you it becomes a then you can start adjusting your budgets. But I tend to think of budget at the it's like it's like an investment portfolio. Like I've got this much amount of money to spend. And where am I likely to invest this money and get the highest rate of return. That's your tier one. But tier one has a certain size and you've exhausted in Google ads. So once you've exhausted, let's just say that you can only spend $90 a day on the average in your tier one market, but you have $200 a day to spend. Well, that's when you go from tier one to tier two is okay, how much could I spend of the remaining $110 in tier two. And maybe you can spend another 90 there. Okay, is it profitable to spend the remaining $20 in tier three, like that's that's the reason we're breaking these things up by geography, if it matters for some of you geography won't matter. But in my experience, geography often matters in funny ways. Sometimes it's obvious. Ecommerce, it's obvious. There is a, I use the phrase geographic affinity, there is a weird geographic affinity effect, where knowing somebody is near you. For whatever reason, I've seen this happen over and over, the likelihood to buy goes up a little bit. I'm just thinking of a client I had for a while, it was a coach. And she lived in Toronto, she did not do one on one coaching, it was all remote. Yet, when she advertised broadly, in English speaking, Canada, she can she converted more people and it nothing spoke to geography in any of her content. But north of 60% of her customers were in Toronto, there was some, and this is representative, I've seen this phenomenon happen a lot. There was something about the fact that she was from Toronto that I don't know why, but it happens over and over again. So geography is your friend. Just know it's a thing that you can lever that you can pull, and then be creative in how you think about pulling that lever. All right, next question, with the time of day, as I am only targeting Europe right now, turn ads off overnight. Again, in the beginning, run ads 24 seven, and they just see if performance drops overnight. See if your conversions go wonky, if you look at a time of day report, like, are there periods of time, it's not always true. The 12 to five example that I use, that's a broad observation across hundreds of AdWords accounts over time that the person eating Cheetos on the couch at 2am is less likely to be such an awful generalization. And I said Cheetos, and I know you're fasting, Andrei, so sorry. But broadly speaking, there seems to be the late at night audiences is probabilistically less valuable. So it's a thing you can use to toggle off, but don't get wound up about it. Do 24 seven for a while, see what happens. Day of week is more of a factor, B to C versus B to B, the business to business. If you have a very clear business to business, in a low quality traffic on the weekends, where it takes 10 times a number of visitors to get the same conversion, just turn weekends off, like these are what's used to bought the broad swings for now and then get much more focused and nuanced over time. Last question from the same person, you mentioned adding date to show urgency, is there something similar we could use for evergreen? That was just that was an example of pulling out something from somebody else's ad, not suggesting using urgency and ads, unless it makes sense. If you if there's urgency in, if there's a way that urgency authentically matters in what you offer, then test it. If there is no urgency and you have to manufacture it, I'm going to say I'm not actually going to render an opinion on that one. I will say that I generally don't like manufactured urgency. At the same time, one of my good friends is Jack Born, a deadline funnel. And one of the things Jack will say is that you need to give people a reason to buy. And Jack is a very sharp guy, and he has lots of data that confirms that. So don't mistake something that I do because I like to do it. And I feel more comfortable not having false urgency with a directive that you should not use false urgency. You should make the decisions about what's best for your business and what you're optimizing for. I'm just that's just not my thing. But I know urgency. I know urgency. And Jack, I've had conversations with me makes a very good point that sometimes urgency is doing the customer a favor that they're they're not making a decision that they really want to make. And by adding some urgency, you're getting them to make a decision that is in their best interest if it's a high quality product. And you're doing well. There's there are lots of reasons to do this. I don't know how much I would I would do a search for like Jack Born podcast or Jack Born interview. I'm sure Jack has spoken about this publicly. He's a super sharp thinker and a wonderful human being. And I go listen to what Jack has to say about urgency, because he probably has forgotten more about that today, that I've learned in my lifetime. And he's a great resource to do that. Okay, last couple of questions. I understand that the best way to test an idea to use Facebook ads instead of Google ads. That's not what I meant. So sorry. Is it because Facebook allows us to reach a virtually infinite audience? No. While Google limits the audience according to the specific keyword searches we decide to work on? No, sort of. I will answer this after I'm not being full up here. Or because Facebook allows us to filter among hundreds of user preferences while on Google, we just rely on a specific specific search query the user inputs in the search bar. Okay. Okay. Lots of things here. The best way to test an idea is not necessarily Facebook ads instead of Google ads. I like to use Facebook ads to cut to test concepts in the expression of ideas, because we have a lot more space to do that. You cannot write 1000 word Google ad. You can't write 1000 word Facebook ad. So that is really that is the primary answer to the question. Now, Google and Facebook are not the same thing. So when we test something on Facebook, there is absolutely no analog to Google. So if you get an incredible result with an offer on Facebook and you think, I'm just going to do exactly what I did on Facebook and I'm going to do it on Google. Number one, it's not possible. Number two, the thinking there is wrong. The intent is entirely different. Facebook passive potential interest enormous audiences where within those audiences at any given time, somewhere in a neighborhood of three to seven ish, maybe 10% of that audience might possibly be receptive to your idea. Like that's, you know, that's, those are sort of the broad strokes. Right. There is a fraction, a small fraction of a big audience that is not actively looking for what you have to offer, but when they see it, might be interested in it. That's, that's the beauty of Facebook. And you can go to that audience with a very long description of what you're thinking and we'll cover how to do this module 6.5, where you can have a different lead. So this grabs their attention. You can have a different hook, which is like the first three or four paragraphs, whatever that sort of the expression of the idea. And you can have a different, I say angle, Lawrence's theme, themes better. I'm trying to retrain myself to say themes. So you have sort of a theme to the whole thing. And you can then test different themes to see in different hooks and different angles. And like, you can, you can test our different leads. You can test those things on Facebook because there's room to test them. You have 650 words, 1000 words, you can really describe something. And you can get lots of nuanced understanding because the platform allows for it, but recognizing that the audience isn't looking for it. You can also test ideas on Google ads. You know, sort of famously Tim Ferriss's book, the four hour work week, the title came from testing he did before the book was titled on Google AdWords. He tested all these different variations and Google, the four hour work week was the one that ended up being the winner that got more clicks or whatever. So there's lots that you can test with Google ads, but you're testing active intent. Somebody wants something. They've raised their hand and said, I am interested in X. So knowing that the nature of the test, it's very different. You're, you can test the intent of a search phrase, like you think somebody searching for X is a perfect match. And you find out that in fact they aren't, or they are, or you could, you can test two different expressions and AB or it's not really an AB test, it would be more like an AZ test, where you can have two wildly different ads that are really about the same thing. But one of them is focused on what you've identified as the most important pain the audience has. And the other is focused on the most important gain. And just for, just for fun, let's do a three part test. The third one is focused, the language is about the most important job you think they have to do. And that will then tell you which of these three things based on response motivates them at the largest section or segment of the audience. Are they primarily motivated by this job, by this pain or by this gain? Or if you have three pains and you think these three pains, like these are the big drivers, test them against each other and I'd work to have three different ads, each one speaks to specifically to one of the pains that you believe is most relevant to your audience. Because then you're going to learn really a few things. You're going to learn which of the three that you've chosen resonates most. And you're going to have a pretty clear idea if the ones that you've chosen are the right ones based on the aggregate of how much attention that you're getting. And you may find that you were right, all three are great. And they convert about equally and that between them, you get a big chunk of the audience. Then you know you've nailed it. You've nailed the pains or the gains or the jobs, whatever. And there are so many ways that you can use Google to test. So don't consider this binary. We only test on Facebook because we only test on Google because we always test. It's just how we test and what we test. That is a little bit different based on the intent of the audience. You know, second my own voice kind of thing? No, no, no. You were you rocking it. Good times. All right. So first question, I'm not sure I understand is a Google ads test. Oh, gotcha. All right, gotcha. So Google has a certification program Google certified advertising professional. Is it worth taking? And is Facebook footprint worth taking? So my answer to both of these is yes. Google, you know, the thing you have to be aware of when you are, when you do any of these trainings is that you are, you're getting the perspective from the advertiser, from the platform, like of how they think you should do it, knowing essentially nothing about your business. So don't necessarily, don't take the class, these classes and don't go through these programs. From the perspective of you're going to learn exactly how to do things, there are a way to learn about the platforms and how the platforms work and how they think about them. But at the end of the day, Google's interest is that you spend lots of money on Google and Facebook's interest is that you spend lots of money on Facebook, that you give their audience is something that they want. But they are optimizing primarily for their economic interests. That's, that's as true as it gets. So yes, you should take these things. But when you do hear like Google will tell you to use BroadMatch, and it's, it's a really, really bad idea, particularly for a new advertiser, to use BroadMatch, don't do it. Like so compare, compare those things, compare what you learn to the frameworks that you're learning from Google and Facebook. I think both of those programs are outstanding from learning how to do the things, but they're, they're not as good about what you should do. And you can do some really interesting stuff with Tag Manager that you can't do just with native tracking, dropping code on a page. Okay, answer. Next question. All right. I've got some keyword campaigns currently set up in a way that looks far from optimal when measured against the guidelines in module three. Lots of keywords up together in an ad group, broad match rather than anything more targeted. But they're currently working well for me. They get people to opt in to my emails and I'm getting between 1.5x and two times return on ad spend after 14 days. What are your thoughts and the benefits of changing the search terms to a better approach versus the risk of messing things up if I make changes to something that's already working? There is actually a YouTube ads campaign, but I'm guessing the principles here will be same for both Google search and YouTube. Maybe. So this is a common question, right? Things are working. I don't want to break what's working. So what do I do? It's a great question. Thank you, Mark. I think what you, the way that I would approach this is less about module three and more about module four. There's sort of two answers to your question. If you don't mind people seeing your account, then let me go in and optimize it and we'll do a screencast and we'll share with everybody and I'll redo the whole thing or at least tell you what to do. That's my favorite thing. And that's one option, but I totally understand if you don't want somebody to see inside your account and I would do search only. I'm not going to monkey around with the YouTube stuff. So what you really are trying to do though is find the signal in the noise. So if you're using broad match, if you go in and look at your search term report, you're going to find that there is a minority of cause creating the majority of effect. And at a minimum, there's sort of two ways to approach it. You can pull out the stuff that's working and make it work better or you can just turn off all the stuff that's not working. So if you go in and find out that you've got a broad match, whatever, three terms in broad match and you think it's producing a set of search terms and in fact it's not, then go find the search terms that are converting, make them phrase match, put them in the same ad group as phrase match, and then go turn off broad match. And then you've turned off all the noise and you've kept the signal. And you've now just probably gone from 1.5 to 2x real as to five or six like immediately. That's just in nature of every account I ever look at. There's a tiny minority of signal. You're trying to find it and amplify it. And the two ways to do that are to focus on the bright spot of what's working and pull that out and put it someplace else and really concentrate on it with better ad copy and other things or to just take something that's already working if you're nervous and just turn off all the stuff in it that's not working. Those are really the two paths. I do them in want to be cautious how I say this because it will sound absolute and it's not. In general, if I do like a fly by account optimization where someone just wants to make it 50% better in 10 minutes, then I will look at an account like that and I will just go in and just like these 10 things don't work and they're not working egregiously bad, turn them off. And you can do that by going into like the search terms report, filter by clicks and then look at conversions and what you're looking for are lots of clicks, low or no conversions. And you'll find that there's some percentage of search terms in there that they generate a lot of traffic and they don't get conversions and making those stop, however it takes to make those stop, is putting out the fire and you should put that fire out first. And you don't have to change, you're not changing anything else, you're just getting rid of the boat anchor around the neck of your account and your row as will probably double, triple immediately just by doing that one thing and you haven't changed anything else. That's a really low risk way to do it. Beyond that, I think the question becomes like, so here's the phased approach. Like if I end up convincing you enough that there is a better way and you want to dip your toe in it, what I would do is take the, let's just assume you, I'll be very simple terms, assume you have like three campaigns that are working and I would create campaign number four that is, that you intend to replace one of your campaigns with gradually and I would then go into that a campaign that was working and I, you know, I structure the campaigns exactly the same. So same geography, same time of day, same everything's the same, your mobile adjustments, your tab, all that stuff is the same between the two. And then I would go into the first, to the campaign that's working and this is, you know, Perry Marshall invented this idea, it's brilliant, he called it peel and stick, which you're peeling the high performers out, you're taking them out of an account, of a, of a campaign or an ad group and you're sticking them into a new one where you can put more attention on it. So I would go into your first, the campaign that's working and I would find the thing that's converting most, what, go to search term report, find the keyword phrase that generates the single largest number of conversions and I would take that phrase and I would go into my new campaign, which you will have paused in the beginning, go into that campaign, set that up, create an ad specifically for that phrase, go through the exact process in module three for that high performing phrase and then once that's set up, turn, nothing else is different, same landing page, just a different copy, same thing, as soon as that's set up, turn it on, turn it off in the first account. You'll have, you know, whatever, a few hours or getting traction, but now you've, you've pulled out a high performer and you've put it in the, the other, in another account and then I, and I would just keep doing that. I would pull out and what you'll probably find is you have, I don't know, I don't know how big your account is, five, six, 10 phrase, search phrases or search, you know, combination of terms that are producing the overwhelming majority of your results and in a day, you can rebuild them as a high performing campaign and then just go turn that first campaign off and now you have a really well structured campaign and nobody got hurt in the process. Like that's how you, that's how you go from one to two times row as to like 15, 20 times row as, like that's the, that's the magic without anyone getting too scared. But if you really, if you, if you want to volunteer, then volunteer and let me do this for your account because this would be fun. All right, next one. All right. I've set up two campaigns for my e-commerce business. Nice job taking action. Keywords around benefits of widget, discovering intent and keywords around buy widget, buy or new intent. What do I think about this approach? Now let me just go through the whole question. What do I think about that approach and how do you think I should go about the buyer intent campaigns landing page straight to the product page or collections page or something else? Okay. So let me answer all the questions in order. I don't like that you've, you've mixed these two things together and I know you didn't mix them together in the same campaign. You've got two different campaigns. I get that, but that you're doing these two things at the same time. Stop doing the first thing, do the second thing. If get people who are looking to buy, to buy, spend all your money on that until you can't spend more money on that. Spend all of your time and your attention on that until you know that's working, then layer on the next thing, the benefits of widget. Do the exact same process. Does that, can you make that work? Is there not the same thing that if you think about a spectrum of buying intent, the people who are, are, have buy language that have shown buying intent are far more likely to actually buy, they're far closer on the spectrum to the act of buying than the benefits crowd. You're, you're having to move the benefits crowd from conceptually further away, closer to buying and that's hard work. That's more work. So having those two things happening at the same time, in general, all things being equal, that concerns me. So that's how I think about the approach to answer your question. So then how do, how do I think about the buyer's intent campaign? So buy, easy answer for this one, when there's buying intent, go straight to the product page. If someone says, I want to buy X, trust them, they want to buy X, show them X, and with a place to buy it, like just trust what they say. If the thing that they want to buy is in a family of things with options, like, you know, I'm thinking like I had a client in the golf space for a long time, you know, someone might be searching for a particular Callaway driver, but they're actually five variants. Well, in that case, send them to the page with five variants, if they don't specify the variant, because then it gets, then it's, you have to kind of show them that this thing that you've searched for that you think is one thing is actually one thing with five variations. So it's five things and here are all five. So that's, that's a service to the customer, to the searcher. But if it's not that, if it's, I'm searching for X and you're like, well, we have X and YZ and Q, I search for X, show me X, right? That's it. It is as simple as that, I think. You're, again, your result may be a little bit different because nuances of your product, but sort of from a framework thinking perspective, I trust when people tell me what they're looking for, and I, I try to give them that and not try to get too crafty in my thinking unless there's a really good reason why I might want to show them something else. This was not asked as a question, but this is also the answer to why I generally don't love this idea of bidding on like competitor terms. Like this is not, I haven't worked with either of these companies, but when somebody's like, you know, hey, I own ConvertKit, when people search for drip, should I advertise? A lot of people like, yeah, absolutely. You know, it's kind of the same thing. I'm like, people search for drip, they're looking for drip, right? It's like, let them, I see this happen with a lot of my clients, like, well, this other company offers very similar to what we do in the same area. So when someone searches for them, shouldn't we bid on their terms? And like, I get it. I understand that, yes, there's sometimes where it does work, but I tend to lean more toward trusting that the searcher is telling you what they're looking for. And it is a service to give them that and only that, or not give them anything if what we have is not that. You're my wish, maybe, right? Okay, next question. Hi, Lizzie. We need to know when, like the velocity, when over time leads are converting, and then the rate at which those leads are converting, and when we know that, we can calculate CPA, even if it takes a really long time. So let's just assume for the sake of argument that those 600 leads turn into 60 customers two years later, we know their CPA is $60, right? Like the numbers are like those are the pieces of the puzzle. So the way to do what you're trying to do is to work backwards from the purchase to find every inflection point where money is spent and have a way to calculate that.
