What if you have a high ticket offer and it's been very successful, but through word of mouth, not paid traffic, how would you then test it online? Same thing. Look for early signals, minimum viable product. If you have an offer, sell it. So you don't need an MVP because you've got an offer. So I guess the question is, do you have the infrastructure of an offer that you can sell online? If you don't have, if you've just sold something offline, and I'm a perfect example of this, the agency side of my business, I have a very successful high ticket offer that I've sold through entirely word of mouth and referrals for a very long period of time that I don't sell with paid traffic and I don't have the infrastructure to sell it online. So how would I go about doing that? If that's the case, if the infrastructure doesn't exist, but you've sold the offer, then I think it is well within your, it's worth your time not to do an MVP to do it right. Figure out why people who've purchased it purchased it. If you need to talk to people through your customers or whatever, you can infer lots of things, but now spend the time to do it right because you know that it's converted. People have bought this thing. That's what matters. That's what really we're trying to figure out. The first thing we're trying to figure out with an MVP, minimum viable funnel, minimum viable product, minimum viable offer, whatever you pick your poison. What we're trying to figure out first and foremost is will people buy the thing? You've answered that question. So now you're at that next tier, which is will they buy the thing if I try to sell it this way? That's a different question, but you've answered the big question, which is people will hand you dollars for your idea. So now you just need to know will they hand you dollars online for your idea? And what's the best way to do that? So a way to approach this, which is kind of the MVP thought is you see this a lot with agencies and other service providers as you do the strategy call funnel kind of approach where you don't build out all of the infrastructure to sell what you do online, which you build that instead is some measure of credibility and authority and you will highlight your experience and you talk about how you've done for others and solved it. And then the next action is that you get people on a consultative call where they're raising your hand and you're then trying to sell them the way that you've sold it in the past already, which is by talking with people. And you may find if the volume is low enough that that's the alpha in the Omega, that's all you need to do. You also might find that it's a different texture of visitors who come and that that model doesn't work nearly as well. And you have to tweak the model and you need to make if especially if you have to high ticket, you may find that what you need to do instead is put a webinar in front of your funnel and make it clear that the service that you're offering is at a certain price point so you don't get on the phone with people who can't afford it. Like there are lots of variations but you've solved the biggest problem which is you've sold it. So now you just have to figure out what you need to test is how do you sell that same thing in a different environment. And then yes, an MVP approach which I think is probably if it's a service, it's probably either constantly some sort of content on the front end and then consultative call or it is content on the front end to an informational either video or webinar and then consultative call or they can buy it from the web. High ticket offers generally are sold by a webinar. That's just sort of the thing that's proven itself. So that's sort of how I would approach that question. All right, next question. I'm having a hard time thinking about the jobs to be done for my offer. It's for a course on helping people get over grief because of the loss of the loved one, feel better, get better back to normal, and quotes, be happy again, seems to surface level. Any recommendations on questions? Get me thinking better about this. Yeah, focus on the pain. Now, when I gotta be cautious, you know, we don't, Facebook in particular does not like pain focused ads. So I say focus on the pain, recognize that jobs, pains and gains are all texture of messaging. And you are probably in your, I'm guessing that you're in a place where people are motivated primarily by wanting the absence of the pain that they feel. You know, I think back, I lost my father in 1999 to cancer. I was not hoping, like when I was thinking about things, I was not imagining myself in the future feeling better. I was just wanting the pain to go away. Like that was my focus. So I suspect your audience, that's the driver, that's the language around it is meeting them where they are, which involves saying, recognizing that this, that they're in a place that's that hurts, and that what they want most is not to hurt. That I suspect that's it. There is the possibility, depending on the nuances of your market, there's the possibility that depending on when this is happening, they are looking it to the future in some capacity. And they're starting to think about what it will feel like to feel good again. If I, if I were knowing what I know just from the what's inferred in the question, I would be less focused on jobs and far more focused on the thing that's right in front of us, which is you are in pain, and we can show you a way out of that pain. And if the reality is, which in my experience, it was the only way out is through, then part of it is being honest on that with that upfront saying, this is not a magical solution. This is, this is a way to start toward feeling better, or not feeling horrible. So that's, that's how I would approach them. And don't feel it for everybody, don't feel like you have to like, you're checking three boxes, have to have jobs or whatever. The beauty of jobs to be done theory, like when you strip away all the goofy intellectual arguments about it, and everybody, you know, trying to rewrite it and figure out what Clayton Christensen really meant and how he was wrong, you get rid of all of that. That the beauty of jobs to be done is just that we, there are lots of reasons we buy stuff, right? I mean, we, and they're not all functional. We buy things to, to, there are functional things that we buy. But there are also things that we buy for emotion and for with a social component, and you know, and others and not to offend anybody on the call if you are a Prius owner. But there is a different, I drive a full size, you know, full, full door, crew cab, Toyota Tundra. People buy Tundras for different reasons than people buy a Prius, yet both of them go from A to B, right? They get us A and back. And yes, maybe there's a little nuance in that about ride quality and extra sure, but same manufacturer, same function, wildly different emotional and social needs fulfilled by that. That's the, that's what we really think about when we think about jobs to be done. Okay. Next question. Hi, Lizzie again. In the screencast, you mentioned how the $5,000 was a terrible overspend. And some of the other ads should be switched off because they hadn't converted under the $800 target CPA. What percentage are you looking at to know when you should switch an ad off? How much more than a target CPA? Okay. So, yeah, so there's no finite answer to this because especially at that amount, one more conversion can make a big difference. So I think the example I used in there, it may have been toward the end where I said, you know, one of them was that I can't remember what it was. It was too high. There wasn't a lot of search volume for it. There's only one conversion, not a lot of volume, not a lot of upside. But if there were one more conversion before that hit the next increment, right? The next increment being double CPA. If there were one more conversion, that was going to work. You know, it's just a low traffic environment. So a rule of thumb that we could probably use here, which I don't know that I've ever thought about before. So thank you, Lizzie, is double CPA probably is the max threshold. And here's why. If we double CPA and we get another conversion right after, I think the math doesn't get us back to CPA quite right. But if we haven't doubled CPA and we get another conversion, then our CPA is below our target. Like that's the math of it. I always say five out of four people are bad at math, but I think that math is correct. But once we've doubled it, we're not going to get back to feeling good about it. You know, we're going to get kind of back to a not so good CPA. So I think my new rule of thumb, 21 years into making apparently, is that double target CPA is the maximum allowable before I'm going to say that one's not working. And I might have to retest that under different circumstances or some nuance, but the 5000 was just such an aberration, like who let that spend that much money when there was no sign it was going to be profitable. That to me just looked like I see this all the time, an account that's running, it's not being looked at nearly frequently enough. There's a keyword in there that's expensive, and it's driving a lot of traffic, terrible combination. And it just someone was asleep at the wheel. They just didn't, they weren't watching that thing. And it went instead of going to 1600, it went to 5700. That's a huge waste of money. And never should have happened. So that's the nuance. The new heuristic rule of thumb that I like, that I will call the Lizzie method from now on is double target CPA, no conversion, turn it off because you're not mathematically going to get back to where you want it to be without some miracle. So that's my final answer for today at least. I like that too. That's a good rule of thumb. Okay, Robin, thanks guys. I'm the test bill. Awesome. Yeah, let us know what the results are. Let me know, Robin. I'm curious. Yeah, yeah. And we can always update the show notes with your answer just to help everybody else out. We weren't, you know, your market stole invisible, you know, we didn't have to reveal that. Okay, anonymous attendee. I'm always hoping an anonymous question is like something way over the top that someone didn't want to be associated with asking. Okay, quick question with what you just said about one page manifesto plus a buy button or plus button at the bottom of page to lead to sales is that button where you capture their email to maybe if you when they buy, you're going to catch you're going to get their email. But I think you could also do the same thing for opt-in. I just again, this is this is where we get I said I wasn't going to give Andre a big head twice, but I'm about to. So this is the beauty of sphere of influence. It's an architecture that supports really good thinking. So the thinking it's supporting here is we want to have enough content that a person has to spend time with it. And then we either want them to click through to more content. So we get a signal and then take an action like opt-in or buy, because now we've got some real texture to the data that we're looking at. Or if we just want quick and dirty, let's get a result. We can do a long page of content. And at the end, ask them to buy something or to give us their email for some further interaction about the thing that we're talking about. And we're not offering a bribe when we do that. That's another of the nuances of sphere of influence that I think is is brilliant is that the signal we pollute our signal when we offer a bribe. And so that if we did the exact same thing that we talked about earlier that Andre articulated and I came out of slightly differently. But at the end of that page, we said, and as our way of saying, thank you, you'll get this free report about XYZ. Now we have no idea if we were on the right track, unless the free report about XYZ is the logical next step from the content that we've put on that page. But we want the signal that's clearer, even knowing it will be less of the signal, it's still comparatively more powerful. Because we've kind of said to people in the buy example, we've done the ultimate, like, here's what I'm thinking about. This is not I'm not going to spend 20 hours trying to every persuasion technique to tell you why this is great. This is what I've done. This is what you get. This is how much it is. Yes or no, like that gives a really clean signal. The corollary that this one step removed is here's what I'm thinking about. This is why it matters. Here's some context for it. Here's what I intend to do next. If you're interested in this, then if you give me your email, I'm going to keep having this conversation about this. That's a strong signal too. That's someone saying, I am interested in this. And then if you get that email, and you send them a personal email and say, you know, I'm trying to really figure this out here, three questions, I'd love to know. I love your answers to these three questions. And nobody answers those questions. That's a really good signal that the interest isn't strong. If 80% of the people answer those questions, that's a really good signal that the that the interest is strong. So much nuance and all of that, but it's all on the same architecture of making things a little bit harder and going contrary to trying to make it so easy that everybody just says yes. We're making it harder for people to say yes. We're breaking every rule of persuasion to make it harder to say yes so that the yes is a really strong signal. Just realize how weird it is to be talking about sphere of influence with Andre on the call who created a sphere of influence. That's interesting. Okay. When do you suggest implementing something like heroes or wicked reports to help with attribution and tracking? Given that you can track and it's all on your own site. So full disclosure, I've mentioned before, I am good friends with Scott and Mike at Wicked Reports. I've worked with Wicked Reports in the past. I love those guys, they're just good people. I love Wicked Reports as a product. I know Scott and the whole team are pouring their heart and soul into a challenge that is very difficult. And I've also used Wicked Reports with many clients and have found incredible insights from that makes a lot of things easier. I think the way I would answer and just caveat, I have no financial interest in Wicked Reports, just FYI. I think the way I would answer this question is make it harder on yourself first so that you understand, like you have to understand how to do things. The harder you make it on yourself to get the insights, the more durable the insights are and the better questions you'll start asking. The beauty and the curse of Wicked Reports is it gives you really good answers, really good answers. You can have, for example, you know, thinking of some ways I've used it in the past, you can have a lead acquisition campaign to a multi-party email series to a purchase in the future and it will show the contributions of all of those things to the ultimate goal. And you can have, you know, it will show you contributions of retarget. It's a really, really powerful tool. At the same time, if you haven't forced yourself to understand how your system works and what drives results, I think you get less benefit to be using a tool like that because it becomes a crutch in that sense. And that may not be entirely true. Like I think there are people who learn, you know, who learn a tool like Wicked Reports and really dive into it and understand how it works and extract brilliant insights from it. So you can do that. I just make it harder first. It sounds crazy, but the harder you make it on yourself within reason in the beginning, the better you are. You start asking really good questions because you realize you don't know how to answer a question and it becomes very visible. And once you understand that you can't answer a question, then that ripples out into everything that you're doing because now you want to be able to answer the question. And when you have a tool that answers so many questions for you, I think it, it dumbs down our motivation and our ability to ask really good questions, I think. So having said that, at some point, tools like Wicked Reports become really valuable. My advice for that tool, in any tool really, in Segmentrix, Wicked Reports, any third-party tool like this, my advice, if you do decide to go down that road, is to spend a little extra time on the front end making sure it will work for your system. That's where that old quote that I'm sure is misattributed to Abraham Lincoln where he said, if I had an hour to cut down a tree, I would spend 45 minutes sharpening my axe. I've seen a lot of people see the promise of Wicked Reports or Segmentrix or other things and just jump into the promise not knowing that there's work on their part to learn how to use the system and that it has to match up well with how their system works. So if you have things like you're, the entire front of your business is online, but the purchase happens with a phone call, you need to have that conversation with whatever tool, with whoever does the setup at whatever tool you're using so that they can come back to you and say, yes, all of the benefits that you read that make you want to buy the tool will work in your specific situation because they don't always do. They don't always, that was a weird sentence. That's not always true. I'll try that instead. And that's very disappointing when you find out you're, or that there, I worked with a client for a period of time who used Wicked Reports and he had an enormous leak in his system that he refused to fix. And it basically made Wicked Reports worthless. And, and the refusal to fix, I still don't understand, but the refusal to fix the leak sort of made everything problematic. I like this idea of getting webinar leads. I wouldn't focus on getting webinar leads. I would focus on the biggest problem people have with webinars is getting people to stay to the end, that and show up. Those are the two big ones. So I would approach it more from the perspective of a holistic webinar service, where you say, okay, first of all, what you're going to do is either acknowledge what they already know or tell them something that they don't know, which is that getting leads is only one part of the challenge. What's really challenging for the webinars is you not only need to get a lead, you need to get them excited about attending the webinar, you need to get them to attend the webinar, and then you need to get them to stay to the end of the webinar where there's an offer and get them to go to the offer page. So what we think our job is, get webinar leads, is really many other things. Right. Lizzie, in terms of attribution, not being possible if you get leads in one location and sell in another, is that still true for a physical sale like dentists? But my thought, my comment on attribution was more when you go from digital place to digital place, the transition from one domain to another, you lose that initial attribution. So the example I'll use, I have a client and we finally solved this, but I have a client in the resort space. Their traffic comes in to their, their domain, but then there's a third party utility where they book rooms. And in the past, we would see how everybody came into the site, but all of the conversions and the reservations engine were associated with their website as the referrer. That's how attribution breaks from digital footprint to digital footprint. When you have a CRM and you know the attribution of the lead, you just put the attribution of the lead in the CRM and then it tracks it through the entirety of the life cycle. Okay. Are we going through this with me in real time? So let me answer these questions separately. This is the sort of the no, no good plan survives contact with reality. In general terms, the structure of the traffic engine is really meant to be the structure of how I would approach an offer or a business when it exists. So when an offer exists, what I realized by a lot of the comments and emails and feedback was that there are a lot of people who don't have an offer yet. So I've tried to do two things in parallel, which may not, it may confuse things. And I think as we iterate towards success with the traffic engine, we may make two paths. So people know that if you have an offer that exists, approve an offer that you've sold online, the best thing to do is to start with Google ads, learn the principles, go through module zero, one and two, understand ad words and run traffic because it has active intent. That's the reason the course is structured that way is I think in general, you should start with active intent because it's the most likely to convert. If you don't get good conversion or if there's something wrong, if you're not quite sure, then I do think it's helpful to go through the audience and offer a masterclass and to go through the exercise and jobs, pains and gains to dial in your messaging, both on the landing pages and where you're selling and in your ads. I think there's value in that. If your ads are converting, then that's less of an issue. Like that's just sort of an add on. However, if you don't have an offer and what my concern was, if you get all the you get to this point of like, okay, we're about to run traffic with Google ads, but I don't even have an offer, right? I have an idea, but I haven't really dialed it in. Well, the audience and offer masterclass is really put there to say, let's get your offer dialed in first. Let's make sure that we can solve that problem and you can always come back to Google ads later. And if you, I think in that situation, the way to do it is to go module zero, one and two, understand sort of the field of play that we're on. You can participate in, you can go through module three and four, or you can just not look at those yet. Do you audience and offer masterclass? Both parts start working on that. You can, you know, modules five and six, you can sort of pay attention to those if you want to, you don't have to. I would probably pay attention to five and six because 6.5 is how to test ideas with Facebook. So what I would like to see you do is from the 30,000 foot perspective is understand the principles that we're talking about for paid traffic, get your offer dialed in, understand how to test it better on Facebook to really dial it in. And once you have, get some results from your testing, let's go back to AdWords and run you through AdWords and Facebook and then retargeting and then come together. So there really are three, three ish paths through here. And I think as we get there and as we get to the sort of the end and then what's next sort of phase two for this first cohort of the traffic engine will be a lot of those sort of nuances about how to go through it depending on the scenario work that you have. If the customers are not searching for specific problem key phrases enough, does that mean the key phrase to be optimized? Does that mean the key phrase we optimize or is that to be taken at demand is less so that we can use awareness type ads? That's a good question, but hard questions. So if customers aren't searching, if you don't have a lot of search volume, that, that just tells you there's not a lot of search volume. And that sounds like the dumbest answer possible, only impact the answer. It doesn't mean there's not a lot of interest. It just means there's not a lot of active interest. And that's common, right? I mean, it's common that you could be like, people buy stuff all the time that they, they weren't actively looking for it showed up. And this looks great. So the only thing it tells you, don't get demoralized, first of all, if you have an offer and it's good, and it contributes value to the world, and then you've, you know, there's an audience that can benefit from it, that you can reach online, you're okay. But that's, that's, that's okay. If you don't find like for me, for me and my offer, one of the biggest challenges has been, there's not a lot of active intent search for it. People don't know how to search for it. It's more, it's a yearning more than it is a search. They want it, but they don't even know how to look for it. It's hard. So I could very easily abandon an idea, because it's, there's just not a lot of active intent for it, or the way people look for it is kind of weird. So, so don't worry about that. Now, when you say use awareness type ads, I want to just be very cautious here, like I would, if your search volume, your very specific search volume is low, then what I would do is I would shift over to Facebook. And when you say if awareness type ads, I would move over to Facebook next. And that that's, that would be my next step. I would not then try to move farther away from specificity on Google ads and say, well, if people are searching for, like it's, if they're not really searching for the, the very specific thing I offer, I'll go farther away from it and try to take them from what they are searching for to my thing. Don't do that. Go to Facebook instead, identify an audience, test your, your, use the stuff you learned in module 6.5, test there, then maybe go back to Google. Google is just too expensive to try to convert low quality traffic. If you can get lower quality traffic for cheap, it's possible, it's rare, but it's possible. If you can do that, we'll do that later. Absolutely. But that's not the next best choice. The first best choice is active intent specific search terms that you, that your offer fulfills. That's the best choice, dollar for dollar. Second best choice is to go find a potentially responsive audience on Facebook and show them your offer, even though they're not looking. Third best choice is to go back to Google and move a little farther away from specificity. So that's the order that I would recommend that you work in. Last, last question is like an easy yes, no.
